sentences.text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
My name is George Ginsburg.
G-I-N-S-B-U-R-G.
George Ewsey Ginsburg.
E-W-S-E-Y, Ewsey.
25th of February, 1923.
Danzig Free State.
D-A-Z-I-N-G.
Mm.
It's a free state.
It's-- It-- it was independent.
F-R-E-E S-T-A-T-E.
Too old.
My prewar life.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was born in Danzig from Russian parents which escaped after the Russian Revolution via Poland to Danzig.
My father's name was Mark.
My mother's name was Sonia.
They escaped via Poland to Danzig, where I was born.
I was born in Sopot, Langfuhr, which is a holiday resort about 40 kilometers out of Danzig, on the beach.
And I was born there only.
But after two years, my parents left Danzig for Berlin.
And I went to school in Berlin, in the state school.
And we had a-- we had a-- a Russian nanny with us who also they took the way.
And she spoke Russian with me all the time while I was in Berlin.
My father learned German.
So did my mother.
My mother spoke French before, and German.
And I was-- I grew up in Berlin.
Well, I went first-- I went first to the state school, and then into a higher school, a high school, called gymnasium.
I was there for one year.
And then I was thrown out of the gymnasium.
Now, the reason was I was not-- my parents were not German.
They were stateless.
They had no nationality because their Russian passport was taken away from them and they were stateless.
And the stateless people were the first one who got thrown out.
That was already in 193-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 7.
Well, I remember being a-- a toddler in 1933, when the-- when about 100,000 Nazis with torches marched through the city.
That was before the final election of Hitler as Reichskanzler.
And as I looked, my father-- he was rather a very tall man.
And tears were rolling down his-- and I said, dad, what are you crying for?
He said, that's the finish of us.
That's the finish of Germany.
And they-- they-- they were singing that the blood shall flow from the knives of the Germans.
Jewish blood shall flow.
That's what-- that's a song.
Like, I know it in German, but it's hard to translate.
And then, well, I went to school.
I had no trouble in school with the children at all.
No.
No not at that stage.
Not when I was small.
Yeah.
One particular friend was Walter, a-- a non-Jew German.
And another one was Peter.
And one was Werner Hirschfeld, who is here in Australia.
We all lived in the same district.
We all lived in 28 Regensburger Strasse, Berlin Charlottenburg, which was a good district like [INAUDIBLE] or-- you know, a very good district.
Yes, they did.
Many Jews lived in that area.
You see, the Jews were-- the Jews in Germany were actually part of Germany.
They did not live particular, you know?
And they were part of the culture and part of everything for-- for hundreds and hundreds of years.
And they were considered-- the German Jews considered themselves as German first, and then Mosaic beliefs, you know, which is Jewish, after.
Very national minded.
And they contributed a lot.
My father was a-- a real estate agent, what you call.
In German, they call Immobilien.
You know, Immobilien.
And he was selling and buying houses.
He was a commissioned agent, a independent commissioned agent, and had connections with other people.
My mother was in the home, looking after me and looking after the home.
My home was a-- a-- a big apartment.
On the first floor, we had two bedrooms-- big bedrooms with high ceilings.
We had a large room, a dining room.
We had two bathrooms.
And we had a-- a beautiful balcony and a cedar door to walk into.
[LAUGHS]
And I had my own room.
And we were, you know-- it's-- it was nice trees in that street and nice people.
Well, my neighbor was a-- a Jewish fellow called Hirschfeld-- Hirschfeld family, who came from the country.
They had stores, big stores.
And the rest was non-Jewish.
And opposite, there were Jewish people.
I-- I used to go on a lot with Jewish people because I knew them.
Some of them are now in England, some of them, Israel, and so on.
And some of them are dead.
But I used to go to a normal state school.
And, you know.
No, it was our apartment.
Belonged to us.
Yeah, she was there.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
No.
Well, I went first-- the first high school I can remember-- because some of them is a bit, you know, faded since events of the war took over.
And the school I went to is Fichte-Gymnasium in Emser Strasse, which was-- we were wearing red student caps.
And the faculty of each [INAUDIBLE] we are studying was depending on the ribbon who was around that cap.
Like, if it was a lilac ribbon, it was going for medicine.
If it was a blue ribbon, it was going for something else.
[INAUDIBLE].
And there were only two-- two-- two Jewish students in the whole class, me and another guy.
In that particular time, I was just studying the basics.
But what I wanted to really do is go to aeronautic engineering.
But we have been suffering a bit-- started to suffer as a Jew in that school.
Well, I tried to be, because the teacher said always, about Ginsberg.
You're so clever.
You're supposed to be so clever a race.
Tell me the answer.
And-- and the whole class was laughing, you know?
And I had to get up and give him the answer.
And then outside, next to me were-- I was sitting with Hitler Youth brownshirts, the shorts and little daggers, going to school.
And that's what I had to put up with.
In that particular time, we were-- we were actually starting to get restricted.
The Jew--
My father used to go out on the main street in Kurfuerstendamm, which was one of the biggest streets in Berlin.
And business was done in coffeehouses, you know?
In huge, big coffeehouses with-- with-- with cake and-- and-- and coffee.
And the real estate agents used to come to one big-- big coffeehouse.
And they used to do business there, selling and buying.
Another coffeehouse was restricted for other traits, and professions, and things like that.
So he used to go out every day into town.
And my mother was home.
And then in the evening, sometime they went out in the theater, or live show, or concert.
Yeah, they did.
My mother was very musical.
She was-- she was studying in the conservatorium in Kiev.
She went to the conservatorium in Kiev when she was 17, 18 years of age.
Well, because the Russian Revolution started in Russia.
And then-- and then they were still young.
And they got out in 1922--
--after Russian Revolution.
Yes, we had Shabbat.
We had the candles and we had the challah.
And we had Shabbat.
And after that, well, that was just the-- the three of us, so-- because I'm the only-- I'm the only son.
I'm the only child in the family.
My grandparents from my mother's side were living in Berlin first.
And then they went out to Czechoslovakia, to Prague.
So we had no family anymore in Berlin.
[INAUDIBLE] '35.
I can't remember.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Naturally.
Oh, we got-- oh, we went to her place, to the nana's place.
I remember that.
But otherwise, I can't, you know--
[LAUGHS] Well, we went there and-- on-- on Shabbat.
And we had a nice Shabbat there.
Or on yontif, I used to wait for my mother.
I used to go with the youngsters.
And on the end of the shul, I bought flowers.
And I waited for my mother to-- to give her the flowers.
They went on-- on-- what would you call it?
On-- on yontifs-- Sukkot, [INAUDIBLE], Rosh Hashanah, and Yom Kippur.
[INAUDIBLE].
Traditional religious.
In the house?
No.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Visiting.
Friends came for dinner parties.
And we went to the other people there.
And there was a family called [INAUDIBLE], which was very affiliated with my dad.
Knew them from Russia.
The only thing that [INAUDIBLE]-- he-- he sort of was trying to hide as a White Russian.
He wasn't, you see.
But he succeeded, because there was a very-- there was a very affiliated White Russian community in Berlin who sympathized with the Nazis against the communists.
But otherwise, we just led a normal, good life.
And I was very happy.
One fellow called Walter.
And I was associated with the Betar.
That's a-- that's a youth organization, a Zionist youth organization, which were at that particular time trying to militarize we-- the-- the Jewish youth.
I'm talking about 1936, '37, '38.
And we were very much militarized and almost copied the Hitler Youth, only-- only in a-- in a different way, because we were wearing dark brown shirts.
So the Nazis thought we were fascist too, so they let us exist.
But the Hashomer Hatzair and others, they were forbidden, because they were communists, you see?
We were calling himself-- we were calling ourselves the National Zionist Youth-- something National Zionist Youth, you know?
And we sent-- a lot of kids went on to-- to Palestine.
What about you?
How involved were you?
And what sort of activities did you get involved in?
Oh, I went to camps, to youth camps, to Jewish youths camps.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I went to meetings.
I went to Jewish meetings, which were there, you know?
They were still going on.
They were still allowed.
And, you know, with the flags and marching, and the blue-white flags, and things like that, and singing songs.
And, you know, just that.
And that was it.
And, you know-- but I couldn't get-- I couldn't get a-- I-- I couldn't get a visa to go to Palestine.
Oh, yes.
We tried.
But you see, members of Betar were not given visas to go--
--because we were a danger to the present government over there, which was under Ben-Gurion, and there was [INAUDIBLE] in that particular time.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
And-- and-- and we didn't get any visas.
So I was hanging around.
Yes, my family did try to leave Germany.
As I said, before, they were stateless.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And [SIGHS] my father had made contact with the Costa Rican consul.
The Costa Rican consul in Berlin were issuing visas-- not only visas, nationalities and passports as well for money, for big money.
But he issued too many, you see?
And then all of a sudden, when we were ready to go to Costa Rica with a visa and a passport, he was arrested.
So he had the visa and passport?
Yeah.
The Gestapo.
[LAUGHS]
He was trying to get the Jews out, you know?
But-- for money, naturally.
But, you know, the whole thing collapsed, so we couldn't go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Where German Jews had opportunity to go, because they had German passport with a J on it-- Jude.
But still, countries like South America gave those passports visas, where England didn't either, because they were afraid-- they were afraid that the-- there was [INAUDIBLE].
Well, you couldn't.
There was not possibility.
Well, have I got memory of Kristallnacht?
Sure I did.
On the 9th of November, 1938, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the nighttime, we heard terrible noises and screaming of people all over Berlin.
They broke all the windows.

What I saw is a red sky of about 20 synagogues being burned, and screaming people, and-- and then-- and brownshirts running through the streets, arresting Jews and hitting Jews, and dragging them out, and plundering, and--
--trucks-- trucks-- on trucks, and away to Sachsenhausen.
In bed.
Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
I got a telephone call from Betar to get outside immediately, and I'll be picked up with a truck.
But the truck was a Betar truck to-- to-- to collect the youth, because we had to run in the shuls [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SOBBING] to save the torot.
And we did run in the burning shuls.
I didn't have any fear then.
I was young.
We ran in and-- and saved whatever we could.
We ran-- we ran in, and it was already burning.
And--
Inside.
Everything.
[INAUDIBLE] Strasse.
Yeah.
It was smoking and black.
But not only me.
There was about four or five of us.
And our leaders were standing there.
We made a chain to pass on things.
The-- the other ones ran right inside, and we were standing halfway.
And they saved a few torot.
On the truck, hiding them.
In the Betar-- yeah, in the Betar-- in the Betar headquarters, which was like offices and premises and things like that.
Well, outside, there were people standing with-- with bars and things like that, and started hitting us.
Yeah.
And some of them were bleeding.
And some of them were falling off.
And we-- we run away.
We run to private houses of where-- where our members lived, and headquarters, and so on.
And then I came home.
Well, they were devastated.
And it was also announced on the radio, you know, that the German people were arising after-- after a certain fellow called Von Rath got shot by a guy called Grynszpan.
And Grynszpan was a Polish Jew in Paris who show the ambassador.
That's right.
Well, we knew about that.
But that was a revenge.
They said that was a revenge of the German people.
No.
No.
Well, we had to get out.
They're trying to save me.
So they made applications, because I had an uncle, which was the-- the brother of my mother lived in Brussels.

Now, after the Crystal Night, there was an announcement that Belgium was taking 1,000 Jewish children, Holland was taking 1,000 Jewish children, and Great Britain was-- England was taking 2,000 Jewish children.
They had to be under the age of 16.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So my father was trying to get me in because we had family, we had connections in Belgium.
So he succeeded there.
And they accepted me on a transport.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Crystal Night was in the 9th of November.
And in December, I remember standing on the Anhalter Bahnhof.
This was a huge railway station.
There were kids all over the place.
And there were nurses from Geneva arriving, from Switzerland.
And they put us red crosses around the neck.
And we got a number and a name.
And I kissed my parents goodbye.
They were standing on the-- on the station.
And they were hustling us into the train.
The train was filled with children.
And there was also Gruene Polizei-- that was the green police, border police-- who was on the train, checking papers and things like that.
We were issued with papers, all of us.
And then the train left the station.
[INAUDIBLE] waving, and saw my-- the last time I saw my father.
About [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six hours?
Blank.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
No.
No.
One girl I could remember.
I used to take her out in Belgium all the time.
[LAUGHTER]
Yeah.
I can't remember her name.
So you met--
And then we-- we arrived in Belgium, on the station, in the nighttime-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Gare du Nord, North Station.
And there were all stalls set up.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there were all Jewish women.
They were all volunteers.
They gave us sandwiches and coffee in the nighttime, and blankets.
And they comfort us.
There were about 200, 300 [INAUDIBLE].
Families.
And then children who had no families, they were adopted to other Jewish families.
Took them in the cars and took them home.
My-- my uncle was there.
He took me home.
And that was it.
It was a bit strange.
I hardly know my uncle.
Almost like a stranger.
He knew me when I was little [INAUDIBLE].
It's my uncle and my auntie.
Not at that stage.
No.
No.
And he had his own house there and his own office.
He was well off.
He was in-- in electrical and electronic, you know, import.
And it was a nice house.
And I had a room on the top, on the rooftop.
And that was it.
And I started to go to school.
I went to a technical school, a purely technical school.
But I couldn't speak French.
So I had to learn very fast French, and I learned it fast.
And I was going to school then.
And I-- again, I had a school cap on and things like that.
I started all over again.
I made a few good friends.
One was very, very wealthy.
He called himself Bernard.
And he was one of the wealthy Jews in-- in Brussels.
And I went to jazz bands and things like that.
You know, I started to get interested in this, and start to get interested in girls, and dancing, and things like that.
And I missed my parents terribly.
Yeah, they wrote.
They wrote.
Oh, they were writing every week.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
But I was begging my uncle to put up a certain fund, money, to get them out, because you could do it at that time.
It was costing a fortune.
That was in '39.
I don't know.
But he didn't-- he was not prepared to put up such a sum.
See, you give it to a guide.
And that guide guides them to the border and smuggles them over the border.
But he didn't want to do it.
So that was one resentment.
And I said, then I get the money.
And I earn-- I pay you off every cent, plus a profit.
And he didn't want to do it.
He was not very nice to me on the end.
Not very nice at all.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah, it was all right, yes.
It was all right.
I was very happy in Belgium.
Different.
It was all different, you know?
I had no pressure.
And then I-- I continued to school-- schooling, naturally.
And then my auntie had a baby.
That was my cousin.
She comes from Estonia.
And her name was Marie Louise.
It was a little baby.
And that was already going into the 1940s.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And on the 10th of May, 1940, we saw aeroplanes in the sky in the daytime.
And people went on the balcony pointing at those aeroplanes.
They were German fighters.
And they were machine-gunning the streets in the daytime.
I saw people pointing at them.
And that was the invasion of the Germans in 1940 onto Belgium.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And people prepared to leave.
People started packing.
They were fighting on the-- on the lines, on the Maginot Line, and they're fighting on the Albert Line.
General mobilization and so on.
All right.
All right.
That's OK.
